Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd block out time most days, usually around 26 hours a week total, and I'd focus a lot on reading and writing things out since that's how I learn best. I kept detailed notes and would rewrite concepts in my own words. The thing is, even with all that effort, I struggled to make it to classes as often as I should have, which definitely hurt me in ways I didn't fully anticipate.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up sometimes. I tried to stay ahead, but when assignments piled up or life got complicated, I'd have to shift things around. Being from a low-resource background means I sometimes had to juggle other responsibilities that messed with my ideal schedule. Still, I always came back to my routine when I could.
Q3: I logged into the LMS pretty frequently, honestly—like 27 times over the course. Usually it was to check for new materials, see if there were announcements, or to engage with discussion posts. I genuinely wanted to stay connected to what was happening in the course even when I couldn't physically be there. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if anyone responded to something I posted.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became kind of automatic for me after a few weeks. I'd wake up, grab my phone or laptop, and just scroll through to see what was new. It became part of my morning routine, almost like checking messages. I think the discussion boards really pulled me in because I liked seeing different perspectives and contributing my own thoughts.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful—I watched over 248 minutes worth. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the content at my own pace. The discussion forums were also valuable because they let me process ideas by writing them out and seeing how others interpreted the same material. Since I'm a read-write learner, anything text-based or that let me engage through writing really clicked for me.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skipped over supplementary readings or optional resources that seemed redundant. If something felt like it was just repeating what I'd already learned from the main materials, I'd move on. Time and energy are limited, especially when you don't have a lot of extra resources to fall back on, so I had to prioritize what felt most essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to what I needed to understand or to the assignments. If it helped me write better notes or clarified something confusing, I was all in. I also valued anything that let me engage with peers or express my own thinking—that made learning feel more meaningful and less isolating.
Q8: Optional activities felt like bonuses rather than obligations, which made them less stressful but also easier to skip when things got hectic. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew they'd impact my grade, so I prioritized them even when I was tired. The optional stuff I did was usually because I was genuinely curious, not because I felt forced.
Q9: Absolutely. A lot of my studying happened offline with printed notes, handwritten summaries, and sometimes textbooks I borrowed. I'd download materials once and then work through them without needing to be online. Given my limited resources, I couldn't always rely on constant internet access, so offline studying was essential for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or rewriting lecture content in my own words, and none of that would show up in the LMS. I'd also discuss concepts with a peer in person—just one close friend I studied with occasionally. That kind of deep, reflective learning doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then continue studying from those notes for days without logging back in. It was more efficient for me to work from my own organized materials than to keep streaming or re-accessing things online, especially with inconsistent internet.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when I felt overwhelmed by competing responsibilities or when I missed too many classes and felt disconnected. Sometimes I'd feel surprised by how quickly things piled up. When attendance dropped, I felt like I was playing catch-up, which made engagement feel more like a chore than something energizing.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS use. Some weeks I had to prioritize other things—family stuff, work, basic survival needs—and the course took a backseat. I'd still try to log in and stay aware of what was happening, but my engagement wasn't always deep during those times. Confusion about certain topics also slowed me down.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and scroll through them but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be tired or stressed about something unrelated, and even though I was technically accessing content, nothing was really sinking in. It felt like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Sometimes it was because I felt unsure about how to approach them, and other times I was just drained. With low resilience, when things got tough, I'd avoid the hard stuff temporarily. I always intended to come back to it, but sometimes I ran out of time.
Q16: Deadlines definitely drove a lot of my behavior. I'd ramp up my LMS activity as deadlines approached, checking for requirements, reviewing rubrics, and making sure I understood what was expected. Without deadlines, I might have explored more freely, but they kept me focused—sometimes too narrowly focused on just getting things submitted.
Q17: Feedback mattered to me when I received it, but honestly, I didn't always get it in time to adjust my approach for the next thing. When I did get constructive feedback, it motivated me to engage more carefully with similar tasks. Positive feedback especially gave me a boost, even if my overall mood stayed kind of low.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation a lot. When things were clearly laid out with specific deadlines and organized modules, I could plan my time better. But when the structure felt loose or expectations were unclear, I'd sometimes disengage because I wasn't sure what to prioritize. Clear structure helped me stay on track.
Q19: The discussion forums and video content made engagement easier because they matched how I like to learn. What made it harder was when there wasn't enough guidance on how to approach certain topics, or when I felt like I was missing context from classes I couldn't attend. The gap between my attendance and the course expectations created friction.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or genuinely engaging with content without producing visible output yet. Sometimes you need to absorb a lot before you can produce something meaningful. Or maybe they're struggling with something—confusion, anxiety, life circumstances—that prevents them from completing work even though they're trying.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in the LMS, but my exam score didn't reflect my effort, which was frustrating. I think some students might be efficient learners who don't need constant LMS interaction, or they might have strong external resources. For me, the disconnect was more about exam performance not matching my assignment work.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend on videos. But they miss the depth of engagement, the thinking that happens offline, the conversations with peers, and the emotional labor of trying to stay motivated. They capture activity but not necessarily learning or genuine understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively processing the material, not just going through motions. It means I care about understanding, I'm asking questions, I'm connecting ideas to things I already know. It's about being invested in my own learning, even when it's challenging. Engagement isn't just about showing up—it's about showing up mentally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with balancing everything, or how surprised I was when my exam score didn't match my assignment efforts. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes, the peer conversations, or the emotional ups and downs of trying to succeed with limited resources. My engagement was real, even when the data couldn't fully reflect it.
